findings.description: Off-by-one in assemble: range(total_chunks - 1) skips the final chunk, so every reassembled file is truncated by one chunk despite is_complete passing.
Path traversal: client-supplied filename is joined into UPLOAD_DIR without basename/sanitization, so a name like '../../etc/cron.d/x.txt' (allowed .txt extension) writes outside the upload directory.
Client-supplied upload_id is embedded unsanitized into the chunk filename, allowing path traversal/arbitrary file write via receive_chunk (e.g. upload_id='../../tmp/evil').
No per-chunk or cumulative size enforcement during receive_chunk; MAX_FILE_MB is never applied before writing, permitting unbounded disk/resource exhaustion (DoS).
file_size_ok validates size only after assemble has already written the full file to UPLOAD_DIR, so the limit is reactive and an oversized file already lands in the destination.
File type is validated only by extension allowlist; no content/magic-byte/MIME check, so a malicious payload renamed to .png/.pdf passes validation.
verify_checksum reads the entire file into memory via f.read() instead of streaming, causing large memory spikes for files up to (or beyond) 200MB.

Shared _sessions dict and per-session received set are mutated without locking; concurrent receive_chunk/is_complete/assemble calls create race conditions and TOCTOU on completeness in a multithreaded server.
Destination is keyed by filename, so two uploads with the same filename silently overwrite each other in UPLOAD_DIR.
assemble has no error handling: a missing/unreadable chunk file raises mid-write, leaving a partial corrupt file in UPLOAD_DIR and the session un-cleaned.
receive_chunk allows re-writing an already-received index with no idempotency/duplicate guard, so a later call can silently replace prior chunk content.
Digest compared with '!=' rather than hmac.compare_digest; not timing-safe (low impact here since the value is an integrity checksum, not a secret).
verify_checksum compares the stored file against a client-supplied hash, providing protection only against transport corruption, not against a malicious client.
No exception handling around chunk file open/write; an IOError propagates and leaves session state inconsistent (received not updated, no cleanup).
makedirs creates CHUNK_DIR/UPLOAD_DIR with default (potentially world-readable) permissions; no explicit restrictive mode set for upload storage.
Optional[str] could be modernized to 'str | None' given the codebase already uses PEP 585 generics (set[int], dict[str, ...]).
